abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Association master policy and unit owner coverage obligations under the Declaration and Bylaws.
abstracts.subtitle: The Association maintains coverage for common areas and shared liabilities, while unit owners must obtain personal insurance for their units and belongings.

blocks.heading: Association Insurance Policies
blocks.text: The Association is required to maintain insurance coverage for common areas and shared liabilities. The Board of Directors manages these policies on behalf of all unit owners.

cards.label: Property Insurance
cards.text: Full replacement value of all common areas and limited common areas — roofs, exterior walls, structural elements. Includes fire, wind, and water damage. Does not cover unit interiors or personal property.

cards.label: General Liability
cards.text: $1,000,000 per occurrence for personal injury and death; $50,000 per occurrence for property damage. Includes cross-liability clause.

cards.label: D&O Insurance
cards.text: Protects Board members from personal liability for good-faith decisions. Covers legal fees, settlements, and governance disputes.

cards.label: Fidelity Bond
cards.text: Covers officers, directors, employees, and property manager against embezzlement, fraud, or financial misconduct. Minimum 50% of annual budget plus reserve fund balance.

cards.label: Workers' Comp
cards.text: Required if the Association hires employees or contractors for maintenance or management.

cards.label: Flood / Earthquake
cards.text: Required if mortgage lenders mandate it, for areas prone to flooding or seismic activity.

sourceText.label: View exact policy language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Insurance Requirements
passages: Property Insurance: Full replacement value of all common areas and limited common areas (e.g., roofs, exterior walls, structural elements). Includes fire, wind, water damage, and other perils. Excludes unit interiors, personal property, or unit owner modifications. The Association and Board are named insured parties on behalf of all unit owners.
Loss Payee: Insurance proceeds are held in trust and used for repairs or reconstruction unless a majority vote decides otherwise.

General Liability: $1,000,000 per occurrence (personal injury & death); $50,000 per occurrence (property damage). Cross-Liability Clause ensures claims by one insured party against another are not excluded.
D&O Insurance: Protects Board members from personal liability for decisions made in good faith. Covers legal fees, settlements, and damages related to governance disputes.

Fidelity Bond: Covers Association officers, directors, employees, and the property manager. Protects against embezzlement, fraud, or financial misconduct. Coverage amount: minimum of 50% of the annual budget and reserve fund balance.
Workers' Compensation: Required if employees are hired for maintenance or management services.
Flood or Earthquake Insurance: Required if mortgage lenders mandate it for natural disaster-related losses.
sourceText.ref: Château Ville Declaration, Sections 15.1, Insurance Coverage

blocks.heading: Your Insurance Obligations (HO-6)
blocks.text: You need personal insurance for everything the Association’s policy does not cover — your unit interior, personal property, improvements, liability, and loss of use.

cards.label: Personal Property
cards.text: Furniture, appliances, clothing, electronics, and valuables inside the unit.

cards.label: Unit Improvements
cards.text: Upgrades made by owners — flooring, countertops, cabinetry.

cards.label: Liability
cards.text: Protection from lawsuits if someone is injured inside your unit.

cards.label: Loss of Use
cards.text: Reimbursement if your unit becomes uninhabitable due to damage.

cards.label: Loss Assessment
cards.text: Protection against unexpected special assessments from uninsured or underinsured Association claims.

cards.label: Personal Liability
cards.text: Recommended $300,000–$500,000 minimum. Covers injury to guests or accidental damage to other units or common areas.

sourceText.label: View exact owner requirements
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Unit Owner Insurance
passages: HO-6 (Condo Owners) Insurance: Covers personal property (furniture, appliances, clothing, electronics, valuables), unit improvements (flooring, countertops, cabinetry), liability coverage (lawsuits from injuries inside the unit), and loss of use (reimbursement if unit becomes uninhabitable).
Loss Assessment Coverage: Protects owners against unexpected charges levied by the Association due to uninsured losses or underinsured claims.
Personal Liability Insurance: Minimum coverage recommended $300,000–$500,000. Protects owners if a guest is injured or if they accidentally cause damage to another unit or common area.
sourceText.ref: Château Ville Declaration, Unit Owner Insurance Requirements

action.label: If damage occurs in your unit
action.title: What to do when you need to file a claim
steps: Document the damage — take photos, note the date, and identify the source.
Determine responsibility — if it’s your unit interior or personal property, your HO-6 applies. If it involves common areas, contact the Board.
File with your insurer — you handle unit-specific losses.
Notify the Association — if damage affects common areas or other units, or if you caused the damage.
Provide proof of insurance — the Board may request it at any time.

blocks.heading: Special Conditions
items: The Board handles all claims, policy renewals, and loss payouts for Association policies.
Insurance proceeds must be used for repairs unless unit owners vote not to rebuild after a catastrophic loss.
The Board has authority to settle insurance disputes on behalf of all owners.
Lenders are notified of major policy changes and have the right to review policies and receive payouts affecting collateral.
If a unit owner is responsible for damage to another unit or common area, they must pay for repairs unless covered by their personal policy.
If an owner fails to carry insurance, the Association may charge them directly for any covered losses they caused.

sourceText.label: View exact provisions
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Insurance Provisions & Special Conditions
passages: Association’s Role: The Board of Directors handles claims, policy renewals, and loss payouts. Insurance proceeds must be used for repairs, unless unit owners vote not to rebuild after a catastrophic loss. The Board has the authority to settle insurance disputes on behalf of all owners.

Mortgagee Protections: Lenders are notified of major policy changes. Insurance must meet lender requirements to secure financing. Mortgage holders have the right to review policies and receive payouts if damage affects collateral.

Owner Responsibility for Damage: If a unit owner is responsible for damage to another unit or common area, they must pay for repairs unless covered by their personal policy. If an owner fails to carry insurance, the Association may charge them directly for any covered losses they caused.

blocks.heading: Policy Requirements & Compliance
items: All policies must be obtained from reputable insurers licensed in Washington State.
Policies must be reviewed annually to ensure adequate coverage and compliance with lending requirements.
Unit owners must provide proof of insurance upon request by the Board.

takeaways: The Association covers common areas, structure, liability, and Board members. It does NOT cover unit interiors, personal property, or owner upgrades.
You must carry HO-6 condo insurance covering personal property, liability, improvements, and loss assessment.
Recommended personal liability coverage: $300,000–$500,000.
Fidelity bond must cover at least 50% of the annual budget plus reserve fund balance.
If you cause damage and don’t carry insurance, the Association can charge you directly.
Insurance proceeds must be used for repairs unless owners vote otherwise after catastrophic loss.

fullText.text: 1. Association Insurance Requirements
The Château Ville Condominium Owners' Association is required to maintain insurance coverage for common areas and shared liabilities. The Board of Directors manages these policies.
1.1 Required Policies
1.1.1 Property Insurance (Fire & Casualty)
Coverage: Full replacement value of all common areas and limited common areas (e.g., roofs, exterior walls, structural elements).
Scope: Includes fire, wind, water damage, and other perils as specified in the policy.
Exclusions: Does not cover unit interiors, personal property, or unit owner modifications.
Named Insured: The Association and Board of Directors are named as insured parties on behalf of all unit owners.
Loss Payee: Insurance proceeds are held in trust and used for repairs or reconstruction unless a majority vote decides otherwise.
1.1.2 General Liability Insurance
Coverage: Protects the Association against claims for bodily injury, property damage, or liability incidents in common areas.
Minimum Limits: $1,000,000 per occurrence (personal injury & death). $50,000 per occurrence (property damage).
Cross-Liability Clause: Ensures that claims by one insured party against another are not excluded.
1.1.3 Directors & Officers (D&O) Insurance
Purpose: Protects Board members from personal liability for decisions made in good faith on behalf of the Association.
Scope: Covers legal fees, settlements, and damages related to governance disputes.
1.1.4 Fidelity Bond / Crime Insurance
Who is Covered: Association officers, directors, employees, and the property manager.
Purpose: Protects against embezzlement, fraud, or financial misconduct involving Association funds.
Coverage Amount: Minimum of 50% of the annual budget and reserve fund balance.
1.1.5 Workers' Compensation Insurance
Required if Employees Are Hired: Covers employees or contractors performing maintenance or management services for the Association.
1.1.6 Flood or Earthquake Insurance (if applicable)
Required if Mortgage Lenders Mandate It: Coverage for natural disaster-related losses in areas prone to flooding or seismic activity.
2. Unit Owner Insurance Requirements
Each unit owner is responsible for obtaining personal insurance to cover items that the Association's policies do not protect.
2.1.1 HO-6 (Condo Owners) Insurance
Personal Property: Covers furniture, appliances, clothing, electronics, and valuables inside the unit.
Unit Improvements: Includes upgrades made by owners (e.g., flooring, countertops, cabinetry).
Liability Coverage: Protects owners from lawsuits if someone is injured inside their unit.
Loss of Use: Provides reimbursement if the unit becomes uninhabitable due to damage.
2.1.2 Loss Assessment Coverage
Covers Special Assessments: Protects owners against unexpected charges levied by the Association due to uninsured losses or underinsured claims.
2.1.3 Personal Liability Insurance
Minimum Coverage Recommended: $300,000 - $500,000. Protects owners if a guest is injured inside their unit or if they accidentally cause damage to another unit or common area.
3. Insurance Provisions & Special Conditions
3.1 Association's Role in Claims & Payouts
The Board of Directors handles claims, policy renewals, and loss payouts.
Insurance proceeds must be used for repairs, unless unit owners vote not to rebuild after a catastrophic loss.
The Board has the authority to settle insurance disputes on behalf of all owners.
3.2 Mortgagee Protections
Lenders are notified of major policy changes.
Insurance must meet lender requirements to secure financing.
Mortgage holders have the right to review policies and receive payouts if damage affects collateral.
3.3 Owner Responsibility for Damage
If a unit owner is responsible for damage to another unit or common area, they must pay for repairs unless covered by their personal policy.
If an owner fails to carry insurance, the Association may charge them directly for any covered losses they caused.
4. Insurance Policy Requirements & Compliance
All policies must be obtained from reputable insurers licensed in Washington State.
Policies must be reviewed annually to ensure adequate coverage and compliance with lending requirements.
Unit owners must provide proof of insurance upon request by the Board.

abstracts.title: Maintenance Responsibilities
abstracts.desc: Unit owner and Association upkeep obligations, emergency procedures, and enforcement.
abstracts.subtitle: Responsibilities for maintaining units, common areas, and limited common areas — ensuring proper upkeep, preserving property values, and preventing disputes.

blocks.heading: What You Maintain
blocks.text: You are responsible for maintaining everything inside your unit and any limited common areas assigned to you. No changes to structural components or common areas without Board/ACC approval.
items: Interior unit maintenance — walls, ceilings, flooring, plumbing, electrical, HVAC.
Appliances and fixtures — water heaters, sinks, toilets, lighting.
Interior painting, repairs, and finishes — wallpaper, tiling, carpets.
Windows, screens, and storm doors (unless stated otherwise in governing documents).
Limited Common Areas assigned to your unit — patios, decks, balconies, and storage units.
Minor repairs that do not impact structural integrity.
Pest control within the unit (unless an infestation affects multiple units).

sourceText.label: View exact responsibilities from governing documents
sourceText.heading: Declaration & Bylaws — Unit Owner Responsibilities
passages: Interior unit maintenance (walls, ceilings, flooring, plumbing, electrical, HVAC).
Appliances and fixtures (water heaters, sinks, toilets, lighting).
Interior painting, repairs, and finishes (including wallpaper, tiling, carpets).
Windows, screens, storm doors (unless stated otherwise in the governing documents).
Limited Common Areas assigned to their unit (patios, decks, balconies, and storage units).
Minor repairs that do not impact structural integrity.
Pest control within the unit (unless an infestation affects multiple units).
Unit owners are prohibited from making changes to structural components or common areas without Board/ACC approval.

action.label: If something needs repair
action.title: How to handle a maintenance issue
steps: Determine responsibility — interior components, appliances, and unit-exclusive systems are yours. Common areas and building systems are the Association’s.
If it’s yours — arrange repairs directly. Get ACC approval for any exterior or structural work.
If it’s the Association’s — report it to the property manager or Board.
If it’s urgent and affects others — notify the Association immediately. You may be liable for damage caused by delay or negligence.

blocks.heading: What the Association Maintains
blocks.text: The Association handles all common areas, building exteriors, and shared systems. The Association may charge owners for damage caused by negligence or misuse.

cards.label: Common Areas
cards.text: Hallways, stairwells, lobbies, entryways, sidewalks, driveways, parking lots.

cards.label: Landscaping
cards.text: Landscaping, irrigation, and outdoor lighting.

cards.label: Amenities
cards.text: Elevators, mailboxes, clubhouse, swimming pools, fitness centers, recreational areas (if applicable).

cards.label: Building Exteriors
cards.text: Roof, gutters, exterior walls, foundation, load-bearing structures, exterior doors (unless designated Limited Common Areas).

cards.label: Shared Systems
cards.text: Plumbing and drainage serving multiple units, electrical and gas systems shared by the building, central HVAC (if not exclusive to individual units).

cards.label: Limited Common Areas
cards.text: The HOA may maintain decks, balconies, and patios but require unit owners to keep them clean and in good repair.

sourceText.label: View full Association responsibilities
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Association Responsibilities
passages: Common Areas & Facilities: Hallways, stairwells, lobbies, entryways. Sidewalks, driveways, and parking lots. Landscaping, irrigation, and outdoor lighting. Elevators, mailboxes, and clubhouse (if applicable). Swimming pools, fitness centers, and recreational areas.
Building Exteriors: Roof, gutters, and exterior walls. Foundation and load-bearing structures. Exterior doors (unless designated as Limited Common Areas).
Shared Mechanical & Utility Systems: Plumbing and drainage serving multiple units. Electrical and gas systems shared by the building. Central HVAC systems (if not exclusive to individual units).
Limited Common Area Maintenance (HOA Discretion): The HOA may maintain decks, balconies, and patios but require unit owners to keep them clean and in good repair.
The Association may charge owners for damage caused by negligence or misuse of common areas.

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: The HOA has the right to enter a unit for emergency repairs to prevent damage to common areas or other units.

pullQuote.text: The HOA may enter a unit for emergency repairs. Owners may be billed for repairs caused by negligence, leaks, or failure to maintain their unit. If an owner refuses necessary maintenance, the HOA may make repairs and assess the costs.
pullQuote.caption: Emergency access and cost recovery

sourceText.label: View emergency provisions
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Emergency Repairs
passages: The HOA has the right to enter a unit for emergency repairs to prevent damage to common areas or other units.
Owners may be billed for repairs caused by negligence, leaks, or failure to maintain their unit.
If an owner refuses necessary maintenance, the HOA may make repairs and assess the costs to the owner.

blocks.heading: How Maintenance Is Funded
items: Regular assessments cover routine maintenance and repairs for common areas.
Reserve funds are set aside for major repairs and capital improvements.
Special assessments may be levied for unexpected repairs (roof replacement, storm damage).
Failure to pay assessments may result in liens or legal action against the unit owner.

blocks.heading: What Happens If You Don’t Maintain Your Unit
items: The HOA may require repairs at the owner’s expense.
If damage spreads to other units, the owner may be held financially responsible.
Fines, legal action, or forced maintenance may be imposed for repeated neglect.

takeaways: You maintain interiors, appliances, and assigned limited common areas. The Association handles common areas, exteriors, and shared systems.
No structural or common area changes without Board/ACC approval.
The HOA can enter your unit for emergency repairs and bill you if you’re responsible.
Assessments fund routine maintenance. Reserves cover major repairs. Special assessments cover the unexpected.
Failure to maintain your unit can result in fines, forced repairs at your expense, or legal action.

fullText.text: Maintenance Responsibilities Overview
Defines responsibilities for maintaining units, common areas, and limited common areas.
Ensures proper upkeep, preserves property values, and prevents disputes over maintenance duties.
The Association handles shared elements, while unit owners are responsible for individual maintenance.
Unit Owner Responsibilities
Each unit owner is responsible for maintaining:
Interior unit maintenance (walls, ceilings, flooring, plumbing, electrical, HVAC).
Appliances and fixtures (water heaters, sinks, toilets, lighting).
Interior painting, repairs, and finishes (including wallpaper, tiling, carpets).
Windows, screens, storm doors (unless stated otherwise in the governing documents).
Limited Common Areas assigned to their unit (patios, decks, balconies, and storage units).
Minor repairs that do not impact structural integrity.
Pest control within the unit (unless an infestation affects multiple units).
Unit owners are prohibited from making changes to structural components or common areas without Board/ACC approval.
Association Responsibilities (HOA)
The Chateau Ville Condominium Association is responsible for maintaining:

Common Areas & Facilities: Hallways, stairwells, lobbies, entryways. Sidewalks, driveways, and parking lots. Landscaping, irrigation, and outdoor lighting. Elevators, mailboxes, and clubhouse (if applicable). Swimming pools, fitness centers, and recreational areas.
Building Exteriors: Roof, gutters, and exterior walls. Foundation and load-bearing structures. Exterior doors (unless designated as Limited Common Areas).
Shared Mechanical & Utility Systems: Plumbing and drainage serving multiple units. Electrical and gas systems shared by the building. Central HVAC systems (if not exclusive to individual units).
Limited Common Area Maintenance (HOA Discretion): The HOA may maintain decks, balconies, and patios but require unit owners to keep them clean and in good repair.
The Association may charge owners for damage caused by negligence or misuse of common areas.
Emergency Repairs
The HOA has the right to enter a unit for emergency repairs to prevent damage to common areas or other units.
Owners may be billed for repairs caused by negligence, leaks, or failure to maintain their unit.
If an owner refuses necessary maintenance, the HOA may make repairs and assess the costs to the owner.
Assessment & Funding for Maintenance
Regular assessments cover routine maintenance and repairs for common areas.
Reserve funds are set aside for major repairs and capital improvements.
Special assessments may be levied for unexpected repairs (roof replacement, storm damage).
Failure to pay assessments may result in liens or legal action against the unit owner.
Consequences for Neglecting Maintenance
If an owner fails to maintain their unit properly:
The HOA may require repairs at the owner's expense.
If damage spreads to other units, the owner may be held financially responsible.
Fines, legal action, or forced maintenance may be imposed for repeated neglect.

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.desc: Architectural Control Committee approval process for modifications to units, common areas, and limited common areas.
abstracts.subtitle: The ACC oversees modifications, alterations, and improvements to ensure architectural consistency, aesthetic integrity, and structural safety.

blocks.heading: What Requires ACC Approval?
blocks.text: All alterations or modifications to a Unit — including changes to flooring, plumbing, electrical systems, exterior elements, or anything impacting Common or Limited Common Elements — require prior written approval from the Board.
items: Exterior modifications — painting, siding, windows, doors.
Structural changes — adding or removing walls, expansions.
Common area modifications — landscaping, fences, decks.
Limited common area modifications — patios, balconies, storage units.
Hard surface flooring installations (if prohibited or regulated).
Any modification visible from the exterior.

sourceText.label: View full requirements from governing documents
sourceText.heading: Declaration & Bylaws — ACC Requirements
passages: All alterations or modifications to a Unit—including changes to flooring, plumbing, electrical systems, exterior elements (such as windows), or any modifications impacting Common or Limited Common Elements—require prior written approval from the Board of Directors.
The ACC oversees modifications, alterations, and improvements to units and common areas.
Ensures architectural consistency and maintains the aesthetic integrity of the condominium.
Prevents modifications that could negatively impact property values or structural integrity.

Unit owners must submit a request before making any changes to: exterior modifications, structural changes, common area modifications, limited common area modifications, hard surface flooring installations, any modification visible from the exterior.

action.label: Before you start any modification
action.title: ACC approval step by step
steps: Submit a written request — include detailed plans, materials, colors, contractor details, and estimated completion time.
ACC reviews within 30 days — the Board may also be involved for major modifications.
Receive decision — the ACC approves, denies, or requests modifications to the proposal.
Do not proceed without written approval — work cannot begin until approval is received in writing.
Inspection may follow — the Board/ACC may inspect completed work to ensure compliance with approved plans.

sourceText.label: View exact approval process
sourceText.heading: Declaration — ACC Approval Process
passages: Submit Request: Owners must provide a written request with detailed plans, materials, and colors. May need to include contractor details and estimated completion time.
Committee Review: The ACC reviews the request within a specified time (typically 30 days). The Board may also be involved in approvals for major modifications.
Decision & Notification: The ACC approves, denies, or requests modifications to the proposal. Owners are not allowed to proceed until written approval is received.
Inspection (If Required): The Board/ACC may inspect completed work to ensure compliance with approved plans.
sourceText.ref: Château Ville Declaration, ACC Approval Process

blocks.heading: Restrictions & Guidelines
items: No unapproved work — any unauthorized changes must be reversed at the owner’s expense.
Maintaining uniformity — changes must be in harmony with existing architecture.
No structural damage — alterations cannot compromise building integrity or impact neighboring units.
Limited common area modifications — owners must maintain any approved changes to patios, decks, or exclusive-use spaces.
Compliance with local codes — all modifications must comply with building codes and HOA regulations.

blocks.heading: Non-Compliance
items: Owners may be required to restore the unit to its original state at their own expense.
The Board may impose fines or legal action for violations.
Any costs incurred by the Association to correct violations will be billed to the owner.

takeaways: All modifications to units, common areas, and limited common areas require prior written ACC approval.
Submit a written request with detailed plans — the ACC reviews within 30 days.
Do not begin work until you have written approval.
Unauthorized changes must be reversed at your expense. The Board may also impose fines.
Changes must maintain architectural harmony and comply with local building codes.

fullText.text: Purpose of the ACC

All alterations or modifications to a Unit—including changes to flooring, plumbing, electrical systems, exterior elements (such as windows), or any modifications impacting Common or Limited Common Elements—require prior written approval from the Board of Directors.
The Architectural Control Committee (ACC) oversees modifications, alterations, and improvements to units and common areas.
Ensures architectural consistency and maintains the aesthetic integrity of the condominium.
Prevents modifications that could negatively impact property values or structural integrity.
Types of Changes Requiring ACC Approval

Unit owners must submit a request to the ACC before making any changes to: Exterior modifications (e.g., painting, siding, windows, doors). Structural changes (e.g., adding/removing walls, expansions). Common area modifications (e.g., landscaping, fences, decks). Limited common area modifications (e.g., patios, balconies, storage units). Hard surface flooring installations (if prohibited or regulated). Any modification visible from the exterior.
Approval Process
Submit Request: Owners must provide a written request with detailed plans, materials, and colors. May need to include contractor details and estimated completion time.
Committee Review: The ACC reviews the request within a specified time (typically 30 days). The Board may also be involved in approvals for major modifications.
Decision & Notification: The ACC approves, denies, or requests modifications to the proposal. Owners are not allowed to proceed until written approval is received.
Inspection (If Required): The Board/ACC may inspect completed work to ensure compliance with approved plans.
Restrictions & Guidelines
No Unapproved Work: Any unauthorized changes must be reversed at the owner's expense.
Maintaining Uniformity: Changes must be in harmony with existing architecture.
No Structural Damage: Alterations cannot compromise building integrity or impact neighboring units.
Limited Common Area Modifications: Owners must maintain any approved changes made to patios, decks, or exclusive-use spaces.
Compliance with Local Codes: All modifications must comply with building codes and HOA regulations.
Consequences for Non-Compliance
If an owner makes unapproved modifications:
They may be required to restore the unit to its original state.
The Board may impose fines or legal action for violations.
Any costs incurred by the Association to correct violations will be billed to the owner.

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Governance structure, elections, authority, and current board composition.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors is the main decision-making body — covering rule enforcement, financial management, maintenance, and legal matters.

cards.label: Board Size
cards.text: Five (5) elected directors after unit owners assume control.

cards.label: Initial Board
cards.text: Temporary Board appointed by the Developer. Owners elect their Board no later than 2 years after recording the Declaration or when 75% of units are sold.

cards.label: Terms
cards.text: 2-year staggered terms: 3 directors in even years, 2 in odd years.

cards.label: Vacancies
cards.text: Remaining Board members may appoint a replacement for the remainder of the term, or a special election may be called.

sourceText.label: View governing document language
sourceText.heading: Bylaws & Declaration — Board Composition
passages: Initially, there is a Temporary Board of Directors appointed by the Developer.
Once unit owners assume control, the Board consists of five (5) elected directors.
Unit owners elect their own Board no later than two (2) years after recording the Declaration or when 75% of units are sold, whichever occurs first.
Directors serve two-year terms. Terms are staggered: in even-numbered years, three (3) directors are elected; in odd-numbered years, two (2) directors are elected.

If a director resigns or is removed, the remaining Board members may appoint a replacement to serve the remainder of the term. If unit owners call a special election, a new director is elected by majority vote.
sourceText.ref: Château Ville Bylaws & Declaration, Board Structure

blocks.heading: How Elections Work
blocks.text: After the Developer’s initial appointment, elections are held at the Annual Meeting. The Developer appoints the initial Board, then unit owners take over.
items: Unit owners elect the Board no later than 2 years after recording the Declaration or when 75% of units are sold.
Elections are held at the Annual Meeting thereafter.
Directors serve 2-year staggered terms.

pullQuote.text: Directors can be removed by a majority vote of unit owners at a special meeting called for that purpose.
pullQuote.caption: Director removal threshold

sourceText.label: View exact removal language
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Removal of Directors
passages: Unit Owners’ Authority: Directors can be removed by a majority vote of unit owners at a special meeting called for that purpose.
Developer’s Authority (Temporary Board Only): While the Developer controls the Board, they can remove or replace any temporary director at will.
sourceText.ref: Château Ville Bylaws, Removal of Directors

blocks.heading: Meetings & Decision-Making
items: Regular meetings held at least twice per year.
Special meetings called by the President or at the request of at least three (3) directors.
Regular and special meetings require at least three (3) days’ notice. Special meetings must state their purpose.
Majority of the Board must be present for a valid quorum.
Decisions require a majority vote of those present.
If all directors are present, no notice is required and any business may be conducted.

sourceText.label: View meeting rules
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Board Meetings
passages: Regular Meetings: Held at least twice per year.
Special Meetings: Can be called by the President or at the request of at least three (3) directors.
Notice: Regular meetings require at least three (3) days’ notice. Special meetings require at least three (3) days’ notice and must state the purpose.
Quorum: A majority of the Board must be present for meetings to be valid.
Voting: Board decisions require a majority vote of those present.
If all directors are present, no notice is required, and any business may be conducted.

cards.label: President
cards.text: Chief executive — presides over meetings.

cards.label: Vice President
cards.text: Assists President and may act in their absence.

cards.label: Secretary
cards.text: Maintains records, meeting minutes, and membership register.

cards.label: Treasurer
cards.text: Handles finances, assessments, and bank accounts.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
blocks.text: Board members serve without pay. However, the Board may approve reasonable compensation for specific work beyond standard duties. Board members are not personally liable for good-faith decisions. The Association must indemnify directors against legal claims unless misconduct is proven.

sourceText.label: View compensation & liability language
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Compensation & Liability
passages: Board members serve without pay. However, the Board may approve reasonable compensation for specific work beyond standard duties.
Board members are not personally liable for decisions made in good faith.
The Association must indemnify directors against legal claims unless misconduct is proven.

blocks.heading: What the Board Can Do
blocks.text: The Board governs the Association with broad authority over rules, finances, maintenance, and legal matters.
items: Enforce the Declaration, Bylaws, and Rules & Regulations.
Manage finances, budget, and assessments — including setting reserves and imposing special assessments.
Hire and supervise property managers, attorneys, and other professionals.
Maintain insurance policies for the condominium.
Oversee maintenance, repairs, and capital improvements.
Adopt, amend, and enforce rules for unit owners and tenants.
Represent the Association in legal matters.
Create committees with at least one Board member on each.
Initiate legal action against rule violators or non-paying owners.

sourceText.label: View full authority from governing documents
sourceText.heading: Bylaws & Declaration — Board Authority
passages: Enforcing the Declaration, Bylaws, and Rules & Regulations.
Managing the Association’s finances, budget, and assessments.
Hiring and supervising property managers, attorneys, and other professionals.
Maintaining insurance policies for the condominium.
Overseeing maintenance, repairs, and capital improvements.
Adopting, amending, and enforcing rules for unit owners and tenants.
Representing the Association in legal matters.
The Board may create committees to assist in managing specific tasks. At least one Board member must be on each committee.
The Board may hire a professional manager but retains final authority over key decisions.
The Board can initiate legal action against rule violators or non-paying owners.
sourceText.ref: Château Ville Bylaws & Declaration, Board Authority

blocks.heading: Amendments
blocks.text: The Board may amend some sections of the Bylaws without owner approval. Other amendments require a 60% unit owner vote. Significant changes to Board authority require Board approval, 60% of unit owners, and lender approval if required by mortgagee provisions.

pullQuote.text: Major amendments require 60% unit owner approval. Changes impacting governance, voting rights, or common area allocations also require lender approval.
pullQuote.caption: Amendment thresholds

sourceText.label: View amendment rules
sourceText.heading: Bylaws & Declaration — Amendments
passages: The Board may amend some sections of the Bylaws without owner approval.
Other amendments require a 60% unit owner vote.
Any significant change to Board authority or governance rules requires: Approval of the Board, approval of at least 60% of unit owners, and lender approval (if required by mortgagee provisions).
Lender Notification: Mortgagees have the right to be notified about budget changes, major amendments, and termination of professional management.
Mortgagee Oversight: Certain changes require lender approval, including abandonment of the condominium structure and altering voting rights or common area allocations.

takeaways: Five elected directors govern the condominium with 2-year staggered terms.
Directors can be removed by majority vote of unit owners at a special meeting.
The Board manages finances, enforces rules, oversees maintenance, and handles legal matters.
Major amendments require 60% unit owner approval. Some also require lender approval.
Board members serve without pay but may receive compensation for specific extra work.
Directors are indemnified for good-faith decisions — no personal liability unless misconduct is proven.

fullText.text: Board of Directors – Overview

The Board of Directors serves as the main decision-making body for the condominium. Its authority covers rule enforcement, financial management, maintenance, and legal matters. The Developer initially appoints the Board, but once unit owners take control, they elect directors to oversee operations. Board members serve without compensation and must act in the best interest of all unit owners.
Structure & Composition
Initially, there is a Temporary Board of Directors appointed by the Developer.
Once unit owners assume control, the Board consists of five (5) elected directors.
The number of directors may be adjusted by the Bylaws and can be changed by the Association.
Election & Term

The Developer appoints the initial Board. Unit owners elect their own Board no later than two (2) years after recording the Declaration or when 75% of units are sold, whichever occurs first. Thereafter, elections are held at the Annual Meeting.
Directors serve two-year terms. Terms are staggered to ensure continuity: In even-numbered years, three (3) directors are elected. In odd-numbered years, two (2) directors are elected.

If a director resigns or is removed, the remaining Board members may appoint a replacement to serve the remainder of the term. If the unit owners call a special election, a new director is elected by majority vote.
Removal of Directors
Directors can be removed by a majority vote of unit owners at a special meeting called for that purpose.
While the Developer controls the Board, they can remove or replace any temporary director at will.
Board Meetings & Decision-Making
Regular Meetings: Held at least twice per year.
Special Meetings: Can be called by the President or at the request of at least three (3) directors.
Regular meetings require at least three (3) days' notice. Special meetings require at least three (3) days' notice and must state the purpose of the meeting.

A majority of the Board must be present for meetings to be valid. Board decisions require a majority vote of those present. If all directors are present, no notice is required, and any business may be conducted.
Officers of the Board

The Board elects officers annually, including: President (Chief executive, presides over meetings). Vice President (Assists President, may act in their absence). Secretary (Maintains records, meeting minutes, and membership register). Treasurer (Handles finances, assessments, and bank accounts).
Board Committees & Delegation
The Board may create committees to assist in managing specific tasks. At least one Board member must be on each committee.
The Board may hire a professional manager but retains final authority over key decisions.
Compensation & Liability
Board members serve without pay. However, the Board may approve reasonable compensation for specific work beyond standard duties.
Board members are not personally liable for decisions made in good faith. The Association must indemnify directors against legal claims unless misconduct is proven.
Assessments & Financial Authority
The Board sets annual budgets and assessments. They determine reserves, approve expenses, and collect fees. If needed, they may impose special assessments.
Amendments
The Board may amend some sections of the Bylaws without owner approval. Other amendments require a 60% unit owner vote.
Any significant change to Board authority or governance rules requires: Approval of the Board. Approval of at least 60% of unit owners. Lender approval (if required by mortgagee provisions).

abstracts.title: Resale Certificate
abstracts.desc: Legally mandated disclosures required for the sale or transfer of any unit.
abstracts.subtitle: A Resale Certificate must be provided to the buyer before the sale of any unit at Château Ville, as required by Washington State law.

blocks.heading: Why It Exists
blocks.text: A Resale Certificate is mandatory for all unit sales under Washington State Condominium Law (RCW 64.34.425). It discloses financial health, assessments, insurance, rules, and compliance details to protect both the buyer and the Association.

sourceText.label: View source references
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Resale Certificate Purpose
passages: Mandatory for Unit Sales: A Resale Certificate must be provided to the buyer before the sale of a unit, as required by RCW 64.34.425 (Section 19.9).
Ensures Buyer Awareness: The document discloses financial health, assessments, insurance, rules, and compliance details to protect both the buyer and the Association (Section 14.8).

blocks.heading: Who Prepares It?
blocks.text: The Board of Directors or the Association’s property manager prepares the certificate. The Association must provide it within 10 days of the request.

action.label: If you’re selling your unit
action.title: How to get your Resale Certificate
steps: Request the certificate — the seller (or their agent) must formally request it from the Association.
Pay the processing fee — the Association may charge a reasonable fee for preparation.
Certificate delivered within 10 days — this is a legal requirement.
Deliver to the buyer before closing — the seller is responsible for ensuring delivery.
Buyer has 5 days to cancel — after receiving the certificate, in writing.

sourceText.label: View preparation requirements
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Sections 12.5, 14.11, 19.7.2
passages: Prepared by: The Board of Directors or Association’s property manager (Section 12.5).
Request Process: The unit seller (or their agent) must formally request the certificate from the Association (Section 14.11).
Timeframe: The Association must provide the certificate within 10 days of the request (Section 19.7.2).

blocks.heading: Financial Information
items: Monthly regular assessments for common expenses.
Any special assessments levied but not yet paid.
Reserve fund balance and planned future contributions.
Confirmation if the seller is delinquent on payments.
Current year’s operating budget and most recent audited financial statements.

sourceText.label: View section references
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Financial Disclosures
passages: Current & Future Assessments: Monthly regular assessments for common expenses. Any special assessments levied but not yet paid (Section 14.1).
Reserve Fund Balance: Statement of reserve funds and planned future contributions (Section 14.5).
Delinquency Status: Confirmation if the seller is delinquent on payments (Section 14.10).
Annual Budget & Financial Statements: Current year’s operating budget and most recent audited financial statements (Section 14.8).

blocks.heading: Legal & Governance
items: Disclosure of any ongoing litigation involving the Association.
Copies of the Declaration, Bylaws, Rules & Regulations.
Summary of Association’s insurance policies and owner requirements.

sourceText.label: View section references
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Legal Disclosures
passages: Pending Lawsuits: Disclosure of any ongoing litigation (Section 12.5.12).
Governing Documents: Copies of the Declaration, Bylaws, Rules & Regulations (Section 24.1).
Insurance Summary: Statement summarizing Association’s insurance policies and owner requirements (Section 15.1).

blocks.heading: Property & Maintenance
items: Planned major repairs or capital improvement projects.
Whether the unit is in compliance with community rules and regulations.
Any violation notices specific to the unit.

sourceText.label: View section references
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Property Disclosures
passages: Planned Major Repairs: Any scheduled capital improvements or major maintenance projects (Section 16.4).
Violation Notices: Whether the unit is in compliance with community rules (Section 10.1).

pullQuote.text: Buyers have 5 days after receiving the Resale Certificate to review and cancel the contract without penalty. Cancellation must be in writing.
pullQuote.caption: Buyer cancellation right — RCW 64.34.425

blocks.heading: Fees & Seller Responsibility
blocks.text: The Association may charge a reasonable processing fee for preparing the certificate. The seller is responsible for obtaining and delivering it to the buyer before closing.

sourceText.label: View fee & responsibility language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Sections 12.5, 14.12
passages: Processing Fee: The Association may charge a reasonable fee for preparing the Resale Certificate (Section 14.12).
Seller’s Obligation: The seller is responsible for obtaining and delivering the certificate to the buyer before closing (Section 12.5).

blocks.heading: Why Compliance Matters
items: Failure to provide a Resale Certificate may delay or void the transaction.
Could expose the seller to legal liability.
Ensures transparency — protects buyers and the Association by providing key financial and governance details before the sale is finalized.

takeaways: A Resale Certificate is required for all unit sales — must be provided before closing.
Includes financials, assessments, legal issues, insurance, and property compliance details.
Buyers have 5 days to cancel after receiving the certificate.
The Association must deliver it within 10 days of request.
The seller is responsible for obtaining and paying for it.
Insurance proceeds must be used for repairs unless owners vote otherwise.

fullText.text: 1. Purpose & Legal Requirement

Mandatory for Unit Sales: A Resale Certificate must be provided to the buyer before the sale of a unit, as required by Washington State Condominium Law (RCW 64.34.425). Reference: Section 19.9 - Amendments to Declaration and Bylaws.

Ensures Buyer Awareness: The document discloses financial health, assessments, insurance, rules, and compliance details to protect both the buyer and the Association. Reference: Section 14.8 - Records & Financial Reports.
2. Who Prepares the Resale Certificate?
Prepared by: The Board of Directors or Association's property manager. Reference: Section 12.5 - Authority of the Association.
Request Process: The unit seller (or their agent) must formally request the certificate from the Association. Reference: Section 14.11 - Certificate of Assessment.
Timeframe: The Association must provide the certificate within 10 days of the request. Reference: Section 19.7.2 - Required Notices to Mortgagees.
3. Required Information
3.1 Financial Information
Current & Future Assessments: Monthly regular assessments for common expenses. Any special assessments levied but not yet paid. Reference: Section 14.1 - Common Expenses & Assessments.
Reserve Fund Balance: Statement of the Association's reserve funds and planned future contributions. Reference: Section 14.5 - Separate Accounts (Reserve & Insurance Accounts).
Delinquency Status: Confirmation if the unit seller is delinquent on payments to the Association. Reference: Section 14.10 - Liquidated Indebtedness.
Annual Budget & Financial Statements: Copy of the current year's operating budget. Most recent audited financial statements. Reference: Section 14.8 - Records & Financial Reports.
3.2 Legal & Governance Disclosures
Pending Lawsuits: Disclosure of any ongoing litigation involving the Association. Reference: Section 12.5.12 - Authority to Institute Legal Action.
Governing Documents: Copies of the Declaration, Bylaws, Rules & Regulations. Reference: Section 24.1 - Amendment by Unit Owners.
Insurance Summary: A statement summarizing Association's insurance policies and owner requirements. Reference: Section 15.1 - Insurance Coverage.
3.3 Property & Maintenance Disclosures
Planned Major Repairs or Projects: Any scheduled capital improvements or major maintenance projects. Reference: Section 16.4 - Restoration (Repairs After Damage or Destruction).
Violation Notices: Whether the unit is in compliance with community rules and regulations. Reference: Section 10.1 - Enforcement of Declaration and Rules.
4. Buyer's Right to Cancel

Cancellation Period: The buyer has 5 days after receiving the Resale Certificate to review and cancel the contract without penalty. Reference: Section 19.7.2 - Required Notices to Mortgagees (Applies to Buyer Protections).
Method of Cancellation: Must be done in writing and sent to the seller or their agent within the allowed timeframe. Reference: Section 25 - Notices.
5. Fees & Seller Responsibility
Processing Fee: The Association may charge a reasonable fee for preparing the Resale Certificate. Reference: Section 14.12 - Security Deposit and Fees for Documentation.

Seller's Obligation: The seller is responsible for obtaining and delivering the certificate to the buyer before closing. Reference: Section 12.5 - Authority of the Association (Obligation to Provide Documents).
6. Importance of Compliance

Failure to Provide a Resale Certificate: May delay or void the transaction. Could expose the seller to legal liability. Reference: Section 14.10 - Liquidated Indebtedness (Outstanding Assessments on Transfer).
Ensures Transparency: Protects both buyers and the Association by providing key financial and governance details before the sale is finalized. Reference: Section 14.8 - Records & Financial Reports.

abstracts.title: Quick Reference
abstracts.desc: Key numbers, deadlines, and thresholds from all governing documents in one place.
abstracts.subtitle: Every important number, deadline, and threshold from across all Château Ville governing document abstracts.

cards.label: ACC Review
cards.text: Typically 30 days for ACC to review modification requests.

cards.label: Board Meetings
cards.text: At least twice per year. 3 days’ notice required.

cards.label: Special Meetings
cards.text: 3 directors can call a special meeting with 3 days’ notice.

cards.label: Resale Response
cards.text: Association must provide Resale Certificate within 10 days.

cards.label: Buyer Cancellation
cards.text: 5 days to cancel after receiving Resale Certificate.

cards.label: Owner Transition
cards.text: Board elected within 2 years of Declaration recording or 75% of units sold.

cards.label: Association Liability
cards.text: $1,000,000 per occurrence (injury/death); $50,000 per occurrence (property damage).

cards.label: Fidelity Bond
cards.text: Minimum 50% of annual budget plus reserve fund balance.

cards.label: Owner Liability
cards.text: Recommended $300,000–$500,000 personal liability.

cards.label: Owner Fails to Insure
cards.text: Association may charge owner directly for any covered losses they caused.

cards.label: Remove Director
cards.text: Majority vote of unit owners at a special meeting.

cards.label: Amend Bylaws
cards.text: 60% unit owner vote (some sections Board-only).

cards.label: Board Authority Changes
cards.text: Board approval + 60% owner vote + lender approval if applicable.

cards.label: Board Quorum
cards.text: Majority of directors present.

takeaways: ACC reviews modification requests within 30 days. Do not start work without written approval.
Association liability: $1M per occurrence. Fidelity bond: 50% of budget + reserves.
Major amendments need 60% owner approval. Some also need lender consent.
Resale Certificate delivered within 10 days. Buyers get 5 days to cancel.
Board elected within 2 years of Declaration or 75% units sold.
If you cause damage and lack insurance, the Association charges you directly.
